title: My First Week in the Dorm
body:
My name is Anna. I am from Russia. Now I live in a dorm in China. My room is small, but it is nice. I have one roommate. Her name is Mei. She is from a small city in China.

Every morning I wake up at seven o'clock. I wash my face and brush my teeth. Then I go to the canteen for breakfast. I usually eat noodles or a boiled egg. The food is cheap and tasty.

In the afternoon, I go to class. After class, I do my homework in the library. I like the library because it is quiet. In the evening, Mei and I talk about our day. She teaches me some Chinese words, and I teach her some Russian words.

At first, I felt a little scared. I did not know anyone here. But now I have new friends. We often walk around the campus together. On weekends, we go shopping or watch a movie.

Life in the dorm is different from home. I share a bathroom with other students. I wash my clothes at the laundry room. It is hard sometimes, but I am learning many new things every day. I feel happy here.

title: A Trip to the Supermarket
body:
On Saturday, I went to the supermarket near my dorm. I needed to buy some food and other things for my room.

First, I took a shopping basket at the door. I walked to the fruit section. I bought some apples and bananas. They looked fresh and cheap. Then I went to the drink section. I chose a bottle of water and some juice.

After that, I looked for snacks. I found some cookies and a bag of chips. I also bought a small bottle of shampoo and a toothbrush, because I forgot to bring one from home.

At the checkout, the line was long. I waited for about ten minutes. When it was my turn, the cashier scanned my things. I paid with my phone. Many people in China use their phone to pay, not cash. This was new for me at first, but now it is easy.

I carried my bags back to the dorm. It was not too far, only a ten-minute walk. My roommate helped me put the food away. We put the fruit on the table and the drinks in our small fridge.

Shopping here is different from my country, but I am getting used to it. Next time, I want to try some new Chinese snacks too.

title: Getting My Student Card
body:
Today I got my student card. I needed this card for many things, so I was happy to finally have it.

In the morning, I went to the international students' office. There were many new students there. We stood in a line and waited. A staff member checked my passport and my papers. She asked for my name, my country, and my major.

After that, she took my photo for the card. I smiled for the camera. Then she gave me a form to sign. I read it carefully, but some words were difficult. My friend Lucas helped me understand it.

The whole process took about one hour. At the end, I received my student card. It has my photo, my name, and my student number on it.

Now I can use this card for many things. I can borrow books from the library. I can eat in the canteen and pay with the card. I can also enter some buildings on campus, like the gym and the computer room.

My teacher said the card is very important, so I must not lose it. I put it in a small pocket in my bag. I feel like a real student now. Tomorrow, I will use my card at the library for the first time.

title: My Class Schedule
body:
This week, I got my class schedule. I was excited but also a little worried, because I have many new subjects.

On Monday and Wednesday, I have math class in the morning. The teacher speaks English, but sometimes I do not understand everything. I write down new words and check them later.

On Tuesday, I have Chinese class. This class is fun because we play games and sing songs. On Thursday, I have English class. We practice speaking and listening. The teacher is very kind and patient.

On Friday, I have physics class. It is difficult for me, but I like the experiments. Last week, we studied simple machines. My classmate Wei helps me with the hard parts.

After class, I usually go to the library to study. I sit with my friends, and we help each other. Sometimes we make a study group before a test.

My schedule is busy, but I am learning a lot. Every day, I understand a little more English and a little more Chinese. My teachers say that with practice, everything gets easier. I believe them, so I keep studying hard every single day.

title: How to Measure Things
body:
In science class, we learn how to measure things. Measuring means finding the size, weight, or amount of something.

We use a ruler to measure length. A ruler shows centimeters and millimeters. My pencil is about fifteen centimeters long. My book is about twenty centimeters wide.

We use a scale to measure weight. My bag with books weighs about three kilograms. An apple weighs about one hundred and fifty grams. The scale shows the number quickly and easily.

We use a measuring cup to measure liquid. Water and juice are liquids. One cup of water is about two hundred and fifty milliliters. We use this in cooking and in science experiments too.

We also use a clock to measure time. A clock shows hours, minutes, and seconds. Class starts at nine o'clock and finishes at ten o'clock. That is one hour.

Measuring is important in daily life and in science. Doctors measure our height and weight. Cooks measure sugar and salt. Engineers measure metal and wood before they build something.

In our next class, we will measure the temperature of water. We will use a thermometer. I am excited to try this experiment with my classmates.

title: Adjusting to a New Rhythm of Life
body:
When I first arrived in China, everything felt unfamiliar. The streets were louder than back home, the food tasted different, and even simple tasks, like buying a bus ticket, seemed complicated. However, after a few weeks, I started to develop a routine that made daily life much easier.

Each morning, I wake up early because my first class usually starts at eight. Before class, I have breakfast at a small stall near my dorm, where the owner now recognizes me and often adds an extra dumpling to my plate. Although I still cannot speak much Chinese, we manage to communicate through simple words and gestures.

In the evenings, I try to explore a different part of the city with my classmates. Last week, we visited a night market, and I tried several dishes I had never heard of before. Some were spicier than I expected, but overall, the experience was enjoyable. My friends from other countries often share similar stories, which makes me feel less alone in this process of adaptation.

What surprised me most was how quickly small habits can change. Back home, I rarely used my phone to pay for things, whereas here it feels strange to carry cash at all. Similarly, I used to avoid public transport, but now I take the subway almost every day because it is fast and inexpensive.

Of course, homesickness still appears sometimes, especially during holidays when my family gathers without me. Nevertheless, I remind myself that this experience is temporary and valuable. Every challenge, whether it is finding the right bus or understanding a menu, teaches me something new about independence and patience.

title: A Weekend of Small Discoveries
body:
Weekends abroad rarely go as planned, and mine are no exception. Last Saturday, I decided to visit a park that my roommate had recommended, but I ended up taking the wrong bus and getting off in a completely unfamiliar neighborhood. Instead of panicking, I decided to treat it as an unplanned adventure.

As I walked around, I noticed a small bookstore tucked between a phone shop and a bakery. Since I love reading, I went inside despite knowing that most books would be in Chinese. To my surprise, there was a small shelf of English novels, and the shop owner, an elderly man with excellent English, explained that many foreign students visit regularly. We talked for almost twenty minutes about literature, and he recommended a novel that had recently been translated.

Afterward, I found a quiet café nearby and ordered a cup of tea while I read the first chapter. The café played soft music, and through the window, I could see people practicing tai chi in a small square. It struck me how different, yet peaceful, this scene felt compared to anything I had experienced before arriving.

Eventually, I found my way back to the correct bus route, though by then it was already dark. My roommate laughed when I told her the story, since she had taken that same wrong bus during her first month too. 

Looking back, I realize that getting lost taught me more about the city than any guidebook could have. Sometimes the best way to understand a new place is simply to wander without a fixed plan, remain curious, and trust that most mistakes can be fixed with a little patience and a smile.

title: Registering at the Dormitory Office
body:
Before international students can move into their rooms, they must complete the dormitory registration process, which can feel confusing during the first few days on campus. I want to describe my experience so that new students know what to expect.

When I arrived at the university, I first went to the international students' office, where a staff member checked my passport, visa, and admission letter. She then gave me a registration form and directed me to the dormitory building. Although the form was mostly in English, a few sections were only in Chinese, so I asked a senior student nearby for help translating them.

After finishing the form, I paid the dormitory fee at a small office on the ground floor. The staff explained that payment could be made by bank transfer or through a mobile payment app, which most students prefer because it is faster. Once the payment was confirmed, I received my room key along with a copy of the dormitory rules.

The rules cover several important points: quiet hours after eleven at night, no cooking inside rooms, and a curfew for entering the building after midnight. At first, these rules seemed strict, but I later understood that they exist to keep the dormitory safe and comfortable for everyone.

Finally, I met my roommate, who had already unpacked most of her belongings. She kindly showed me where to find the laundry room, the water dispenser, and the nearest convenience store. Within a few days, the registration process, which had initially seemed overwhelming, started to feel like a distant memory, replaced by the excitement of settling into a completely new environment.

title: Joining the Class WeChat Group
body:
One of the first things our class monitor did was create a WeChat group for all new students in the foundation program. At the time, I did not fully understand how important this group would become for daily campus life.

Every morning, teachers post announcements in the group, such as changes to the classroom, updates about assignments, or reminders about upcoming exams. Because messages appear instantly, students respond much faster than they would to an email. When our math teacher once changed the classroom just twenty minutes before class, everyone still managed to arrive on time thanks to the group notification.

Besides official announcements, the group is also used for practical questions. If someone cannot find the library or does not understand a homework problem, they simply post a message, and classmates usually reply within minutes. I have asked several questions myself, ranging from how to print documents on campus to which canteen serves the best breakfast.

At first, I felt nervous about writing in English inside the group, worried that my grammar mistakes would be noticed. However, I soon realized that most students, regardless of their nationality, made similar mistakes, and nobody seemed to mind. In fact, the atmosphere is generally supportive, with classmates often helping each other correct assignments or share study notes before exams.

Beyond academic matters, the group has also become a place for organizing social activities, from weekend trips to birthday celebrations. Looking back, I believe that joining this group on my very first day was one of the most useful decisions I made, since it connected me to both the practical information and the community I needed to succeed.

title: A Simple Experiment with Water
body:
During our foundation science course, we recently conducted a simple experiment to observe how temperature affects the speed at which sugar dissolves in water. Although the experiment itself was straightforward, it helped us practice several important laboratory skills that we will need throughout the program.

First, our teacher divided the class into small groups and gave each group two identical beakers. We filled one beaker with cold water and the other with warm water, making sure that both beakers contained exactly the same volume, measured carefully with a graduated cylinder. Next, we added one teaspoon of sugar to each beaker at precisely the same moment and started a stopwatch.

As we watched, the sugar in the warm water began to disappear noticeably faster than the sugar in the cold water. We recorded the exact time it took for the sugar to dissolve completely in each beaker, then repeated the experiment twice more to check whether our results were consistent. Interestingly, although the times varied slightly between trials, the warm water consistently dissolved the sugar faster than the cold water did.

Afterward, our teacher asked us to explain why this happened. Working in our groups, we discussed how heat increases the movement of molecules, which allows the sugar particles to separate and spread through the water more quickly. Writing this explanation in our own words, rather than simply copying it from the textbook, helped many of us understand the concept far more deeply.

Finally, we presented our results to the rest of the class using a simple graph that compared the dissolving times. This experiment, though basic, demonstrated how careful measurement, repetition, and clear recording of data form the foundation of almost every scientific investigation we will encounter in future courses.

title: Renegotiating Identity Abroad
body:
Moving to a country with an entirely different language and cultural rhythm forces a subtle but persistent renegotiation of identity, one that rarely announces itself dramatically but instead accumulates through countless small adjustments. In my own case, this process became evident only in retrospect, once I noticed how differently I behaved compared to the person I had been just a few months earlier.

Initially, my strategy for coping with unfamiliarity was to minimize disruption wherever possible: I sought out foods that resembled home, gravitated toward other international students, and avoided situations that might expose my limited language skills. While this approach offered short-term comfort, it also delayed the deeper adjustment that eventually proved unavoidable. It was not until a minor crisis, missing an important bus connection because I misread a schedule, that I was compelled to rely on strangers, gestures, and fragments of vocabulary to solve a problem entirely on my own.

What followed was a gradual, if uneven, shift in confidence. Tasks that had once seemed insurmountable, negotiating prices at a local market, interpreting a menu without pictures, explaining a minor medical issue to a pharmacist, became almost mundane after repeated exposure. Crucially, this shift was not merely linguistic; it reflected a broader recalibration of what I considered normal, acceptable, or even desirable. Habits I had never questioned at home, such as an aversion to eating alone in public, quietly dissolved without my fully realizing it.

Equally significant was the discovery that adaptation is rarely linear. Some weeks brought a rush of newfound competence, while others reintroduced a disorienting sense of foreignness, often triggered by something as trivial as a misunderstood joke or an unfamiliar social convention. Rather than viewing these setbacks as failures, I gradually learned to treat them as recurring, expected features of long-term cultural immersion rather than signs of inadequate progress.

Ultimately, what strikes me most, looking back, is not the specific skills I acquired, but the extent to which the very process of struggling, adjusting, and occasionally failing reshaped my sense of who I am, revealing an adaptability I had not previously known I possessed.

title: The Unspoken Curriculum of Daily Life
body:
Beyond the formal instruction that international students receive in classrooms, there exists what might be called an unspoken curriculum, a set of lessons absorbed not through textbooks but through the accumulated friction of ordinary, everyday interactions. Grocery shopping, navigating public transport, and even queuing at a canteen counter turn out to be surprisingly rich sites of learning, precisely because they demand improvisation rather than memorized responses.

Consider the seemingly trivial act of paying for a meal. In many contexts, this transaction is so automatic that it barely registers as a cognitive task. Abroad, however, it can involve deciphering an unfamiliar payment app, calculating currency conversions under mild social pressure, or interpreting a cashier's rapid instructions delivered in an unfamiliar accent. None of this is explicitly taught, yet competence accumulates rapidly through repetition, often within a matter of weeks.

What makes this unspoken curriculum particularly demanding is its unpredictability. Unlike a syllabus, which outlines topics in advance, daily life presents challenges with no forewarning: a sudden change in bus routes, a closed shop replaced by an unfamiliar one, or an administrative form that requires information one does not immediately possess. Students who thrive tend not to be those with the strongest formal language skills, but rather those willing to tolerate ambiguity and treat repeated small failures as informative rather than discouraging.

Interestingly, this process also reshapes expectations about efficiency and patience. Tasks that might take minutes in a familiar environment, such as registering for a library card or resolving a billing question, can stretch into lengthy, multi-step ordeals involving several departments and unclear procedures. Over time, however, most students develop a more elastic sense of time, learning to treat bureaucratic delays as an unavoidable feature of institutional life rather than a personal obstacle.

Ultimately, this hidden layer of learning, largely invisible to those who have never left their home environment, may prove just as consequential for long-term success as anything covered in formal coursework, since it cultivates precisely the resilience, flexibility, and resourcefulness that sustained life abroad inevitably requires.

title: Navigating Institutional Bureaucracy on Arrival
body:
For most incoming international students, the initial encounter with university bureaucracy proves considerably more disorienting than the academic coursework itself, largely because administrative procedures rarely follow an intuitive or universally recognizable logic. Understanding this reality in advance can significantly reduce the frustration that many newcomers experience during their first weeks on campus.

The registration process typically begins with document verification, during which staff cross-check a student's passport, visa category, and admission materials against an internal database. While this step might appear straightforward, discrepancies frequently arise, an expired document, a name transliterated differently across two systems, or a missing stamp, any of which can delay subsequent steps considerably. Students who arrive with photocopies of every relevant document, rather than relying solely on digital copies, generally navigate this stage with markedly less difficulty.

Once document verification is complete, students proceed to dormitory allocation, health screening, and the issuance of a student identification card, each managed by a different office, often located in separate buildings across campus. This fragmentation, though occasionally exasperating, reflects a broader institutional logic in which specialized departments handle narrowly defined responsibilities rather than consolidating services into a single point of contact. Recognizing this structure, rather than resisting it, tends to accelerate the overall process considerably.

Language presents an additional layer of complexity, since certain forms and instructions remain available only in Chinese, even within programs conducted primarily in English. In such cases, most students rely on senior peers, informal translation applications, or bilingual staff members stationed specifically to assist international arrivals. Universities increasingly recognize this gap and have begun introducing orientation sessions explicitly designed to walk new students through each bureaucratic stage before problems arise.

Ultimately, while institutional bureaucracy rarely inspires enthusiasm, approaching it with patience and methodical preparation, rather than frustration, transforms what initially seems like an opaque obstacle course into a manageable, if occasionally tedious, rite of passage that every successful student eventually completes.

title: Classroom Etiquette Across Cultures
body:
One of the more subtle challenges facing international students in an English-medium foundation program involves not the language of instruction itself, but the unfamiliar and often unstated conventions governing classroom behavior, conventions that can differ substantially from those a student previously took for granted.

In many educational traditions, students are expected to listen attentively, take detailed notes, and reserve questions until an explicitly designated moment, typically at the end of a lecture. In other contexts, however, interrupting a professor mid-explanation to seek clarification is not merely tolerated but actively encouraged, viewed as evidence of engagement rather than disrespect. Students accustomed to the former approach frequently misjudge the latter as inappropriate, hesitating to interject even when genuinely confused, which can compound misunderstanding as a lecture progresses.

Group work introduces a comparable set of unfamiliar expectations. Whereas some students have been trained to defer to whoever appears most confident or vocal within a group, others come from backgrounds that prize consensus-building and equal participation, occasionally resulting in visible friction during the first few collaborative assignments. Over time, however, most students report that navigating these differences, rather than avoiding them, becomes one of the more valuable, if unplanned, outcomes of studying alongside classmates from dozens of different countries.

Assessment culture similarly demands recalibration. Students who previously associated a single, high-stakes final examination with academic rigor sometimes struggle initially with continuous assessment models that weight class participation, quizzes, and smaller assignments throughout a semester. Conversely, students accustomed to constant evaluation may find a heavily exam-weighted course unexpectedly stressful, since it offers fewer opportunities to recover from an early misstep.

Ultimately, foundation programs designed for international cohorts increasingly acknowledge these disparities explicitly, often devoting orientation sessions specifically to clarifying expectations around participation, feedback, and academic honesty. Recognizing that such conventions are neither universal nor self-evident, rather than assuming shared understanding, represents an essential first step toward building a classroom environment in which students from vastly different educational backgrounds can genuinely thrive together.

title: Introduction to Functions
body:
Before we proceed to more advanced material this semester, it is worth pausing to establish a precise and intuitive understanding of what mathematicians mean by the term function, since this concept underlies nearly everything we will study in the coming months, from basic algebra through calculus and beyond.

Informally, a function can be described as a rule that assigns to each element of one set, which we call the domain, exactly one element of another set, referred to as the codomain. The word exactly deserves particular emphasis: a relationship in which a single input could correspond to multiple outputs does not qualify as a function, regardless of how naturally it might otherwise seem to behave. Consider, for instance, the relationship between a person's height and their possible weight; because a given height could correspond to many different weights, this relationship fails to constitute a function in the mathematical sense.

By contrast, consider the rule that assigns to each real number its square. Every input, whether positive, negative, or zero, produces precisely one output, satisfying the defining requirement of a function without exception. We typically denote such a function using notation like f of x equals x squared, where x represents an arbitrary input drawn from the domain, and f of x denotes the corresponding output.

It is useful, especially early in your studies, to visualize functions graphically. When plotted on a coordinate plane, a function's graph must satisfy what is commonly called the vertical line test: any vertical line drawn through the graph should intersect it at no more than one point. This visual criterion offers an intuitive, if informal, way of confirming whether a given curve genuinely represents a function or merely a more general relation between two variables.

As the semester progresses, we will encounter increasingly sophisticated classes of functions, including polynomial, exponential, and trigonometric functions, each governed by distinct algebraic properties yet unified by this same foundational definition. Developing fluency with this concept early will prove indispensable, since virtually every subsequent topic in this course, and indeed in most quantitative disciplines you will encounter at university, depends fundamentally on the language and logic of functions.
